talk_title: Immunological considerations and B7H3-targeted radioligand therapy mechanisms

targets.dependency_context: 4IG isoform overexpressed in prostate, breast, colon, lung, gastric cancers

models.name: CT26 (murine colorectal carcinoma transduced with human 4IG B7H3)
BetaBART Phase 1/2
models.genotype_or_key_features: first‑in‑human enrollment, dose 100 µCi Y‑90 MIL‑33B

clinical_data.population: IR‑resistant CT26 tumors in immunocompetent mice

MOA_summary: BetaBART is a humanized anti‑B7H3 antibody with 20 pM affinity for the tumor‑specific 4‑Ig isoform; when labeled with Y‑90 it delivers beta radiation that induces DNA damage, immunogenic cell death and a CD8‑dependent in situ vaccination, leading to long‑term tumor control and immune memory in murine models.
key_findings: MIL‑33B (Zr‑89) specifically localizes to 4IG‑expressing tumors in vivo, with loss of signal in B7H3‑KO or excess cold antibody controls.
A single 100 µCi Y‑90‑MIL‑33B injection yields 56% long‑term survival in IR‑resistant CT26 tumors, whereas controls die.
CD8 T‑cell depletion abolishes the survival benefit, indicating CD8‑mediated adaptive immunity is essential.
Treated mice develop immune memory, rejecting rechallenge with the same tumor cells at >100 days post‑treatment.
Cold (non‑radiolabeled) MIL‑33B antibody at high dose shows no anti‑tumor effect, confirming radiation drives the response.
Small‑molecule NM600 requires checkpoint blockade to achieve long‑term survivors, highlighting modality‑specific immune priming.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Human 4IG isoform is absent in standard mouse models, requiring transduction; circulating soluble 2IG isoform may act as a decoy sink for the antibody.

combination_strategies.rationale: Radioligand‑induced immunogenic cell death may synergize with checkpoint blockade to enhance CD8 T‑cell activity.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased long‑term survival and immune memory compared with radioligand alone.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combining beta or alpha emitting NM600 with checkpoint blockade overcomes lack of single‑agent efficacy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Long‑term survivors observed only in combination arms; actinium‑225 more effective in immune‑resistant melanoma.

open_questions: Can the 4IG‑specific antibody be engineered to avoid the soluble 2IG decoy and improve tumor delivery?
What is the optimal isotope (beta vs alpha) and dosing schedule to maximize immune priming while minimizing lymphocyte toxicity?

talk_title: Granzyme‑B targeted PET imaging to monitor immune activation during PSMA‑RLT plus pembrolizumab

targets.dependency_context: activated CD8 T‑cells releasing granzyme B in tumor microenvironment

biomarkers.name: Granzyme B PET signal
biomarkers.finding: Lesions with increased SUV correlated with PSA declines and later tumor response in mCRPC patients

models.name: Phase 1 PSMA‑617 + pembrolizumab with granzyme PET
models.genotype_or_key_features: metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer, schedule 1/2 dosing

models.name: Syngeneic tumor models (e.g., MC38, CT26) with checkpoint blockade

clinical_data.value: increase in SUV of 8 bone lesions (April scan) and further intensification on August scan
clinical_data.population: same patient, lesions confirmed as cancer by PSMA PET

clinical_data.value: new lesions became PET‑avid on second scan, matching PSA decline

MOA_summary: A Cu‑64‑labeled GRIP peptide is proteolytically activated by granzyme B released from cytotoxic T cells; the activated probe inserts into nearby membranes, retaining signal on PET and enabling non‑invasive visualization of immune‑mediated tumor killing during PSMA‑targeted radioligand therapy combined with pembrolizumab.
readouts: flow cytometry of granzyme B expression
key_findings: Granzyme‑B PET signal appears in bone lesions after sub‑ablative PSMA‑617 (Pluvicto) plus pembrolizumab, coinciding with PSA reductions.
Serial granzyme PET scans show increased SUV in existing lesions and emergence of new PET‑avid lesions, reflecting evolving immune activation.
Granzyme PET uptake is absent in control mice lacking granzyme B, confirming probe specificity.
Probe uptake correlates with peripheral blood granzyme B induction and with clinical response, suggesting a pharmacodynamic biomarker for RLT‑IO combinations.
No significant off‑target uptake observed in most patients; occasional uptake in sarcoma of unclear relevance.

combination_strategies.rationale: Sub‑ablative radioligand may reprogram tumor microenvironment to enable granzyme‑B release from CD8 T cells.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced granzyme PET signal and PSA decline when administered before or concurrent with pembrolizumab.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade amplifies CD8 T‑cell activation, increasing granzyme B release detectable by the probe.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher response rates in schedule 1/2 cohorts versus schedule 3 (pembrolizumab first).

open_questions: What is the optimal timing of granzyme‑B PET relative to radioligand dosing to capture peak immune activation?
Can granzyme‑B PET predict responders early enough to guide treatment adaptation?

talk_title: Immunostimulatory radioligand therapy to enhance checkpoint blockade and CAR‑T efficacy

targets.dependency_context: expressed on multiple solid and liquid tumors; also on lymphocytes
high B7‑H3 expression in neuroblastoma models
multiple tumor types including B78 melanoma, 4T1 breast cancer

models.name: B16 melanoma
models.genotype_or_key_features: GP100 antigen for CAR T studies

models.name: SK‑N‑AS neuroblastoma (NSG)
models.genotype_or_key_features: adrenergic vs mesenchymal subtypes, VLA4 expression

MOA_summary: Low‑dose targeted radionuclide therapy (e.g., NM600 or Cu‑67‑LLP‑2A) delivers localized radiation that modulates the tumor microenvironment, increases pro‑inflammatory cytokines and CD8 infiltration, and can serve as a lymphodepleting conditioning regimen for checkpoint blockade or CAR‑T cells, while sparing systemic lymphocytes when using small‑molecule agents.
readouts: flow cytometry for CD8, CD4, exhaustion markers (TIM3, LAG3, PD‑1)
key_findings: NM600 at 50–100 µCi (2.5–5 Gy) combined with checkpoint blockade yields ~50% complete responses in B78 melanoma, whereas either monotherapy is ineffective.
In 4T1 metastatic breast cancer, low‑dose NM600 alone controls primary tumor but not micrometastases; combination with checkpoint blockade controls both primary and metastatic disease.
Whole‑body 2.5 Gy radiation abrogates the synergy, indicating systemic lymphocyte depletion impairs efficacy, whereas tumor‑restricted dosing preserves benefit.
Cu‑67‑LLP‑2A enhances lymphodepletion, improving engraftment and persistence of adoptively transferred T cells (Flucy conditioning) and boosting CAR‑T efficacy in B16‑PMEL and neuroblastoma models.
Copper‑67 conditioning reduces exhaustion markers (TIM3, LAG3, PD‑1) on CAR‑T cells and shifts tumor‑associated macrophages from M2 to a less suppressive phenotype.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Full‑length antibody radioligands (e.g., GD2‑Ab) cause systemic lymphodepletion and lack synergy with checkpoint blockade, highlighting the importance of preserving peripheral immune cells.

combination_strategies.rationale: Radiation‑induced immunogenic cell death primes tumors for checkpoint‑mediated T‑cell activation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression and increased cytokine milieu; loss of effect when CD8 cells depleted.

combination_strategies.rationale: Cu‑67‑LLP‑2A provides lymphodepleting conditioning and tumor microenvironment remodeling to enhance CAR‑T expansion and reduce exhaustion.
combination_strategies.phenotype: 5/7 mice achieve complete response versus 0/7 with CAR‑T alone; increased CD8/T‑cell memory markers.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combining Cu‑67‑LLP‑2A with standard chemotherapy conditioning further improves donor T‑cell engraftment.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher donor T‑cell chimerism and tumor control.

open_questions: What is the optimal radiation dose and timing to maximize immune priming while minimizing systemic lymphocyte loss?
Can VLA4‑targeted radionuclide therapy be safely translated to humans for CAR‑T conditioning without excessive off‑target toxicity?
